Aconcagua summit — 6,962 m

Almost two weeks on the mountain for about 30 minutes on the summit.

Three nights above 6,000 m were enough to understand what high altitude really means. Breathing became work. Eating became optional. Sleeping became nearly impossible. Every simple action required planning and patience.

Carrying loads between camps was one of the hardest parts. Repeated carries from 4,200 m to 5,500 m and finally to 6,000 m meant moving slowly, conserving energy and accepting that progress is measured in meters, not kilometers.

Summit day started at 04:00.

From Plaza Colera at 6,000 m the route climbed past Independencia, crossed the exposed traverse and continued up the Canaleta — a long section of loose rock that feels endless above 6,500 m. Progress reduced to two steps followed by ten breaths.

The summit appeared close for hours but never arrived quickly.

At 14:40 the cross marking the highest point of South America finally appeared through fog and wind. No celebration, just exhaustion, tea and silence at 6,962 m.

After about 30 minutes it was time to descend before the weather worsened.

The way down proved nearly as demanding as the climb. Strong wind, low visibility and extreme fatigue required full concentration all the way back to camp.

Three nights above 6,000 m were enough — for now.

Harder than Kilimanjaro. Harder than expected. But unfinished business with high mountains never really ends.
